text.text: *In addition to using the name America, we will also refer to the continent as Abya Yala, a term used by the indigenous peoples of the continent*

### Introduction

This project, launched on <htdate0>, represents over a decade of dedicated research and six years of marital collaboration. It stands as a testament to both teaching and learning, and I anticipate not only updating the instructional content but also expanding recognition to those who have contributed to this endeavor.

Family

In a genealogical project, gratitude cannot be limited to immediate ancestors alone, as our existence is intricately woven with the fabric of all who came before us. Therefore, I extend my gratitude to individuals of Afro, Jewish, Christian, Muslim, royal, impoverished, and various other societal classifications that collectively form the mosaic of our Homo sapiens lineage.

To my family, wife, and daughter, I seek the reader's understanding as I express my thanks in the manner to which they are accustomed:

Ninety years ago in Sabará town was born,
A person, through whom poetic craft was worn.
Time wouldn't let him see with his eyes,
Yet all his teachings with his family shall arise.

Poetic license lets me turn the stanza's frame,
Though indigenous values, which inspire like a flame,
Are kept alive, so all may know and understand,
Spreading the end of indifference across the land.

My love for science left my soul not fully filled,
Knowledge helped me see how evolution was built.
But still a void within me longed to be complete;
Luckily, indigenous bonds made our union sweet.

Your patience and devotion in our shared embrace,
Remind me of my grandpa, who taught me love and grace.
Each day I spend with you brings wisdom we can share,
To help us raise our daughter with a lifelong care.

For everything, I'm grateful!
Forever by your side,
My heart feels so fulfilled,
Though ingrate I may hide.

This poem serves as an apology to the individual who has supported my personal growth as a person and continues to assist with this project.

### Teachers

I am grateful to the teachers and professors I have encountered, from kindergarten through my master's degree, who instilled in me a love for science

Genealogy and History

The scientific endeavor benefited from the contributions of various individuals, whose support made this project possible:
  • I express my gratitude to Dr. Vinicius de Oliveira Godoy for his invaluable lessons in the field of human sciences, as he helped me rectify misconceptions I carried over from the exact sciences. Do you remember when I mistakenly believed that "truth" remained static over time?
  • I extend my thanks to Vinicius da Fonseca for providing genealogical data from his research, which played a crucial role in confirming alterations to the history of a common ancestor.
  • To my cousin Saulo Franco, whose insightful genealogical blog () and published works on our family have been instrumental. Our discussions have been instrumental in rectifying errors in our research. Thank you for inspiring me to create this project.
  • I've come to understand that genealogy cannot solely rely on documentation in today's context. This insight was imparted by the geographer, local historical researcher, and genetic genealogist Celito Regmendes. Additionally, I'm indebted to the valuable lessons provided by Eduardo Santos-Lima. Thank you teachers!
  • After years of studying physics and other sciences, I found myself lacking in certain grammatical concepts. I've also noticed shifts in previously acquired knowledge, as language is dynamic. While some texts may contain grammatical errors, one thing remains certain: I've significantly enhanced my linguistic prowess, all thanks to my dear cousin Adalberto Camargo. Thank you!
  • We all fall into the trap of complacency at times, and having someone to challenge us is a blessing. Over the past years, my dear cousin Eduardo Olifer has been instrumental in shaking me out of this state, contributing to our collective knowledge of our ancestors. Thank you!
  • Upon the passing of my beloved cousin, Avelina Maria Noronha de Almeida, our family lost a historian who illuminated our history. The impact of her absence was profound, initially dissuading me from continuing her work. However, months later, a message from genealogist Gilber Rubim Rangel on WhatsApp changed everything. His invitation to contribute to GENEA - Revista Capixaba de Genealogia reignited my passion and determination. Thank you, Gilber!
  • No individual can possess all available knowledge, and receiving assistance in this pursuit is invaluable. The resources utilized in constructing this project are a culmination of lessons learned from various individuals, some of whom I've already acknowledged, and others I list below:
    • Thank you to teacher Maximiliano Pessôa for consistently providing me with books and genealogical trees that have proven immensely helpful.
    • I'm also grateful to Suzana V. Stucka for her thoughtfulness in remembering my lineage to the Leme family and providing material from an unexplored region.
  • Preserving documentation of our ancestors is always a pleasure, but paper can deteriorate over time. Accomplishing this on a large scale requires more than just infrastructure and dedication. Therefore, we are sincerely grateful to the Tribunal de Justiça de Minas Gerais ( ; ) and COARPE - Coordenação de Arquivo Permanente () for their invaluable work in this endeavor.
  • To enslave another person is one of the most tragic events in human history, and we must not forget it. We are thankful to the Centre d'Estudis, Debats i Tertúlies for cataloging these events and inspiring us to acknowledge this in the Americas (Abya Yala) ().
  • A family is more than a group of people sharing DNA. We also share knowledge and common histories. It is always an enriching experience to connect with other relatives, even those genetically distant, who are equally committed to preserving and celebrating our heritage. We are deeply grateful for the support provided by Wil Lem and the Lem Family Association in helping us educate others about the Lem family ().
  • I am deeply grateful to Karolyne Damasceno Brito for her contributions at various moments, such as suggesting the reorganization of the lateral menu and asking insightful questions that helped us enhance the content of History Tracers.
.

source.text: Tribunal de Justiça de Minas Gerais
Linktr. Tribunal de Justiça de Minas Gerais

text.text:
Museums and Archaeological Sites

Regarding the archaeological sites I have visited and the museums I have contacted:
  • The sacred city of Caral is one of the oldest archaeological sites known in America (Abya Yala) at the moment I am writing this expression of gratitude. The discovery of millennia-old artifacts in this city is helping us redefine our understanding of Indigenous societies. I express my sincere gratitude to all the people working to maintain and conserve this remarkable city ( ; ).
  • Depending on the region of the world we are in, it can sometimes be difficult to access discoveries about human history. I am grateful to the Ashmolean Museum for its efforts in conservation, exhibition, and for sharing images that help us teach and understand human history ( ; ).
  • I am deeply grateful to the British Museum for many reasons, particularly their dedication to conservation and exhibition. Their sharing of images and knowledge has greatly enriched our understanding, especially regarding online content ().
  • Although I am still just beginning my studies in areas like Egypt and Elam, I have learned a great deal in a short time, and this is due not only to academic articles but also to the Louvre Museum. I am grateful to the Louvre Museum for preserving all the heritage it holds, as well as for the online materials and references that allow us to deepen our knowledge. And, of course, I cannot fail to thank them for allowing the use of their materials for educational purposes ().
  • Nowadays, where time is increasingly scarce, it becomes necessary to find well-structured and updated websites and books that allow us to understand certain topics. We are immensely grateful to Fran Dorey and the entire team at the Australian Museum for the incredible work done in explaining how our species and related species are connected and emerged over time ().
  • Maintaining art objects is a challenge for different reasons. Explaining, in an organized manner, historical events so that people can understand the context in which the pieces were created is more than a job; it's a true testament to the love of knowledge. Finding people who are capable of doing all of this and still sharing with the public what they do is a true honor. We thank the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York for everything it is doing to keep knowledge accessible, alive, and exposed to the population ().

source.text: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York

text.text:
Indigenous and researchers

On the topic of our Indigenous heritage and history:
  • I've had the privilege of visiting various archaeological sites across America (Abya Yala), also known as Latin America (Abya Yala). These journeys introduced me to Indigenous communities, who, despite being distant relatives, imparted invaluable wisdom. In recent months, I've developed a close relationship with Ludwig Nguillatun, a member of the Mapuche Indigenous community, with whom I engage in almost daily conversations. He has been a driving force behind the creation of the "Who ...?" section, where individuals learn to contemplate diverse topics. I am deeply grateful to him for his inspiration.
  • The desire to write about our Indigenous heritage has been with me for over a decade, yet uncertainty surrounded the approach. Thankfully, this year, , Roberto Moras, creator of the channels America: Lo que no te contaron and Crónicas Prohibidas de América ( ; ), began sharing content about Indigenous cultures and established a platform for communication. Through our discussions over the past few months, I've gained clarity on how to proceed with this project. Rather than viewing it as competition, I see it as a complementary effort to enrich his work. I extend my sincere appreciation to Roberto Moras for his invaluable contributions.
  • While our initial research focused on the American (Abya Yala) continent, is committed to treating all people equally, thus we cannot overlook other continents. We are immensely grateful to Cy for sharing knowledge about other regions of the world on his channel ().
  • After decades of exclusively working with numerical and mathematical methods originating from Eurasia, in we were introduced to a new methodology developed by the Incas: the 'Yupana Inka', and its application through the "Yupana Inka Tawa Pukllay" method. Firstly, we would like to extend our congratulations to Dhavit Prem () and Yupanki Association for their remarkable scientific research, and naturally, we express our gratitude for sharing this knowledge with the world ().
  • The Mesoamerican Indigenous play a significant role in shaping this project. While numerous papers have been published about them, there remains a vast expanse of their history waiting to be uncovered. I extend my heartfelt congratulations and gratitude to Orlando Josué Casares Contreras (), José M Gamboa Cetina, Venus Sarahí Estrella Cabrera (), and the team at INAH-MX for their invaluable research and dissemination efforts concerning these cultures.
  • Due the fact I am from exact sciences, I recognize I am still in adaptation to work with some data from Humana sciences, this conduct me to be assertive when I should be reflexive. I am thankful to Roberto Palomino for your help to identify in some texts the mistakes I had.
  • Understanding the past, especially in contexts lacking documentation and chronicles that describe how people lived, is always a challenge. In such situations, contributions from specialists in diverse fields are essential. For this reason, we extend our congratulations and gratitude to Dr. Ximena Catepillán, whose work has significantly enhanced our understanding of the mathematics of Mesoamerican Indigenous cultures ().
  • Although Indigenous cultures are extremely rich and possess an admirable history, information about them is still scarcely disseminated in schools. This highlights the need to create institutions that broadly promote this knowledge and legacy. We admire and are grateful to Mexicolore for its efforts, over the past decades, in disseminating the culture of Mexico and the Maya, which made possible the development of some of our tools ().

source.page: Roberto. AMERICA : LO QUE NO TE CONTARON

text.text:
### Africa and its Researchers

Often, when people hear about Africa, one of the few things that comes to mind is Egypt. They might also know about peoples who inhabit or once inhabited regions of the Sahara, but Africa is more than that. Africa is the cradle of humanity and has a vast region below the Sahara that is still understudied, with much to reveal about us today.

- We extend our heartfelt thanks to Dr. Wilbert Zvakanyorwa Sadomba for his published studies, which not only served as a source for some texts but also as motivation and inspiration for the study of Bantu peoples, especially in the Zimbabwe region. His knowledge, his line of reasoning, and his permission to use images have been invaluable to this project. Thank you very much, Dr. Wilbert Zvakanyorwa Sadomba (<htcite0>).

- One of the great surprises when studying Zimbabwe and East Africa was realizing how information about the trade between these peoples and the Chinese is underexplored, or even ignored, in much of the Western world we have encountered. This became even more evident in the richness of detail, organization, and structure of Dr. Bing Zhao's research. It is true that, at this point, China remains underexplored in History Tracers, but the seed for this change was planted by the work of Dr. Bing Zhao, who also kindly allowed the use of her images for educational purposes. Thank you very much, Dr. Bing Zhao! Very soon, the seed planted by her work will sprout into a tree of knowledge in this project (<htcite1>).

### Asia and Its Researchers

As we grow up in the West, Asia appears at certain moments in our lives, even when our ancestors came from there, as is the case for Indigenous peoples of the Americas (*Abya Yala*). In light of this, we turn to various publications and some of their authors to bring knowledge from that continent and connect it to our own.

- Asia is a continent where monsoons and skillful agricultural management enable the feeding of billions of people, allowing the trees necessary for our sustenance to sprout and grow. Similarly, the amount of knowledge gained through the work of Dr. Bing Zhao caused the seed initially planted during our study of Africa to grow into a tree of knowledge, extending toward Asia. Once again, we thank the doctor for the inspiration her work provided (<htcite0>).
- The wealth of knowledge originating from a single region of the planet, already substantial, grew even greater when we came into contact with another expert, Professor Xiaohuan Zhao. Initially, the analogy we drew between the names America (*Abya Yala*) and China (Zhongguo 中國) was not only confirmed but was so thoroughly detailed that it became a reference for all pages where China (Zhongguo 中國) is mentioned. Thank you very much, Professor Xiaohuan Zhao (<htcite1>).
- Maintaining knowledge and documents that are beyond our borders is generally a challenge, for various reasons, such as language. Allowing that collection to become available to other people makes your efforts even greater, because, in addition to having to translate the documents, it may be necessary to interact with people who possibly also do not have as deep a knowledge as yours. We are enormously grateful to the Tohoku University Main Library (<htcite2>) for maintaining the collection and for the kindness with which they collaborated in sharing knowledge.
- Due to the dimensions of the planet, getting to know all the cultures and places over a lifetime becomes extremely complicated, to say the least. When we come across organizations that share not only their culture but also materials that help us better understand everything that has been developed over millennia, we are led to admire not only the people but also their culture. We are grateful to the Japanese Society (<htcite3>) for everything they have done, allowing us to bring different content to History Tracers.

### Agencies and Institutes

Many research projects are only developed when countries invest monumental amounts of resources. And when the results reach the general public, we see incredible discoveries that help us evolve as a society.

- We are deeply grateful to ESA (<htcite0>) for all its research and for allowing us to use its images to illustrate the content of *History Tracers*.

Free Software

This open-source project utilizes code from various other open-source projects, as well as contributions from organizations and individuals who share their code with the community. Without this collaborative effort, the project wouldn't have reached its current stage, and for that, we extend our gratitude to:
  • The developers of VIM (), whose support for languages like HTML, Python, and JQuery greatly simplifies the lives of developers.
  • The developers of GIMP () and LibreOffice () for creating tools that assist us in creating and enhancing our images.
  • When we required a web server quickly and efficiently, there was no hesitation in our choice of language. We're grateful to the developers of Python (), who not only support this project but also aid numerous researchers. We are also thankful to the developers of the RJSmin (), NumPY (), Matplotlib (), and RCSSmin () modules for the monumental help their modules provide.
  • To all those involved with the OpenJS Foundation (), for streamlining our work on the WEB.
  • The developers of Chart.js (), whose contributions help us demonstrate the connection between mathematics and our lives in a user-friendly manner.
  • To John Walker and Fourmilab (), for sharing algorithms that enable the conversion of dates between different calendars. Thanks to these algorithms, readers can delve into the realities of other cultures.
  • To the developers of various web browsers, whose work enables people to access and engage with the project's content.
  • Grammar is always evolving, and it is often difficult to keep up with these changes when you work in a different field. We are grateful to OpenAI and DeepSeek ( ; ) for developing models that help us improve our writing..
  • We are thankful to W3Schools () for teaching new web technologies and sharing codes that simplify our development.
  • Images enhance the learning process in an amazing way. Therefore, we are grateful to Font Awesome for sharing a version of the project for free ().
  • Inclusive education is only possible when there is a deep understanding of people's needs. In addition, it is essential to have tools that enable us to connect with them. We are thankful to the developers of Piper () for creating a tool that allows us to convert text into audio.
  • Understanding your own history is an incredible way to explore different sciences, but creating your own genealogical tree is not a simple task. We are grateful to the developers of Gramps for creating an open-source tool that helps us achieve this goal ().
  • Software development goes beyond just writing algorithms. Among the languages chosen for this project, Go deserves special mention for its libraries and cross-platform compatibility ().

Online documentation and images

History is a science; therefore, we cannot simply memorize and accept facts without scrutiny. It is essential to have access to information for a better understanding. Different websites help readers to better comprehend history by providing historical documents and books. We are grateful to all the sites mentioned in History Tracers for sharing this valuable information.

  • We are grateful to Sveinbjörn Þórðarson for maintaining the Icelandic Saga Database online (), which enabled us to create a level in our Scientific Method game.
  • Sometimes, merely describing an object cannot evoke the same passion or impression as being able to see or touch it. I am deeply grateful to Émilie Pagé-Perron and the entire Cuneiform Digital Library Initiative () team for sustaining a project that brings us closer to human history.
  • Sometimes, when we read a text, it’s not easy to picture the regions of the world being described. We are deeply grateful to Stefan Fischerländer for creating the Mapswire () project, which provides maps that help us teach science.
  • The separation between myths and facts is achieved through the use of documentation and chronicles preserved over centuries. We are deeply grateful to the Portuguese government and the institutions Biblioteca Nacional de Portugal and Torre do Tombo for their dedication to preserving and sharing knowledge with the general public ( ; ).
  • Given the impossibility of visiting museums around the world, it is always positive when we find people who, during their visits, photograph and share their images, in addition to striving to provide information to people from various parts of the world, allowing them to also experience the same.
    When we most needed information about the Paleolithic, we were introduced to the work of Don Hitchcock, who, besides allowing us to expand our knowledge on the desired topic, authorized the use of his incredible images in this project to teach you, further increasing our admiration. Thank you very much, Don Hitchcock ().

### External Support

To maintain our focus on teaching and research, it is inevitable to rely on the assistance of specialists in other areas of knowledge. Given this scenario:
- Having a company does not just mean obtaining a registration. There are several steps, not only to register it, but also to keep it running. And all these steps, when shared, alleviate the pressure to achieve results. We thank Carlos, Lúcia, and the team at *Inovatec Contabilidade* for all the administrative support provided over more than half a decade of partnership (<htcite0>).
- To ensure that you had free access and did not forget where to go to see the content on *History Tracers*, it was necessary to register the trademark. This is a process that does not happen overnight, requiring, among other things, organization and attention to people from those responsible. For all of this, we thank Bruno Alves and the entire *Direção Marcas* team for their assistance in this registration journey (<htcite1>).
